I will not be returning.

I have never seen a more expensive amusement park that is run so badly. They should pay people to go here. You can legit spend a grand for a family of four and get on four or five rides.

Layout? Idiotic - three distinct paths that don't connect. Go down one and you are trapped. Pathways and signs? Enjoy going in circles when the few paths that are there are closed off.

Food? Enjoy virtually no options with bang up prices like 5.50 for a bottle of water. Took a half hour to get a pulled pork sandwich and fries on a Tuesday at 3PM.

They understaff, under run the rides to save on maintenance, and generally don't care. Go literally anywhere else.

Update - after pretending they care I was supposedly going to at least get money back for the "fast passes," but they ghosted me. The owners of this place suck.

The trams weren't running, and the line to get in stretched all the way back to the first tram stop in the main parking lot. The most frustrating part? The parking lot wasn't even full. There is no reason to have families baking in the Florida heat in a single, massive line other than total mismanagement.
The "customer service" was the final straw. My family had to step out of the long line to use the restroom, and upon returning, we were told to restart at the very back of the line. To treat long-term, loyal supporters this way is a slap in the face. We will no longer be pass members. Busch Gardens has become a disorganized mess that clearly doesn't value its guests.

Busch Gardens in Tampa is a nice place but so expensive. I would not go again unless I had free tickets again. $147 +$45 parking. Um...nope.
That said, the park itself is nice. Large, directing paths to attractions, lots of benches and some shady areas.

The lines for the roller coasters (like 8 I think) were so long and annoying. We rode one ride and the train, then chose not to bother with the rest. There are no small coasters for anyone thats a scaredy cat like me; however, there is a kid area that looks cool. We basically walked around looking at animals. It's like an amusement park and zoo combined.

Parking lot is huge with decent size spaces. It took almost 45 min to get to the parking booth due to traffic. There is a tram from the parking lot to the park, but we walked the 2+ blocks with no issues.

Overall, a nice place if you like coasters and animals. Nice to walk around and see the cool art too.

As a first-time visitor to Busch Gardens Tampa Bay, I was excited to celebrate my niece's 17th birthday here. I snagged my ticket on Groupon (definitely the best bang for my buck), but unfortunately, the experience didn't quite match the hype.

I must have picked an off day because 5 out of 11 rides were closed. One ride had a posted 25-minute wait, only to shut down while we were standing in line. Major letdown. My ticket included a meal voucher, but with only two restaurants open in the entire park, food lines were extremely long. Guest Services wasn't any better -- the line was just as packed with other frustrated guests sharing similar complaints.

I will say, the manager was professional and listened to my concerns, ultimately providing a voucher to visit one of their sister parks, which I appreciated. Still, spending $16 on parking and hours wandering around with limited attractions open made us question if we should've just enjoyed a free beach day instead.

Also worth noting: Adventure Island wasn't open, as staff were reportedly training at Busch Gardens for the upcoming season.

Hoping for a better experience next time at a different park.

I went to Busch Gardens for their Mardi Gras celebration and was severely disappointed. It was very clear that this was another one of their low budget events. In the past I've gone to their Christmas Town event and it was pretty pitiful. This time, the Mardi Gras area was decorated minimally and the performers who came through really misrepresented New Orleans. They were not coordinated and the dance style was more Latin than NOLA.

My partner and I also decided to try their beignets but only had enough for one serving available. Mind you, the event started at noon and we stopped by the food both at 2:30. They initially offered a refund but then took it upon themselves to give us a piece of king cake instead. The king cake wasn't bad, unlike the beignets that tasted like chewy donuts. My partner also got a hurricane and it was very bad.

Prior to stopping by the Mardi Gras celebration, we went to the food cafe. I got fries which were room temperature at best and my partner got a cheeseburger. It took an extremely long time to prepare and when he got it, the cheese wasn't even melted. Busch Gardens should really think about partnering with a reputable company to provide their food services.

I'm not sure who is managing the budget but it needs a serious review! Busch Gardens is good for roller coasters and looking at the animals, but the events are so underwhelming!

Some of us haven't been to Busch Gardens in a while so we decided to take advantage of an opportunity to go just in time for the Christmas Town celebration.
While there, we also purchased the Christmas Town Food and Beverage Samplers lanyard.

Like SeaWorld, the food and beverages samplers are not available until 4pm. I hope the parks decide to change the start times next year to at least 2pm. After being in the park all day, we were hungry and ready to try the samplers. We did have to purchase snacks and drinks in the meantime.

My favorite samplers were the Prime Rib with Potato Puree (no horseradish sauce), the Lamb Chop with Potato Puree and the Apple Spiced Cheesecake. The Prime Rib and Lamb were flavored and had a great taste. The Apple Spiced Cheesecake was creamy and rich but not too sweet. The Spiced Apple Slices were so good and complimented the cheesecake well.

There were a few team members that were amazing:

Frances at the gate was welcoming and in good spirits.

Sebastian at Dragon's Den was delightful. He made the best boba drinks. The Seeker's Keeper boba drink was delicious.

Dominic took the best photos. He was professional and had a great attitude. The photos came out great.

Sade at the Photo Key counter helped with a photo issue. She was patient and took her time to look up and locate the account details needed. Her assistance and expertise help correct the problem.

The park was decorated in festive fashion. There were plenty of photo stations throughout the park. We also participated in the Photo Scavenger Hunt by finding all the stations and submitting all our photos online.

By the time we got there, almost all of the roller coasters were already closed. Only one was open and it had more than an hour wait. While we were waiting, it broke down, which added 30 more minutes. After all that, the ride itself didn't feel like much. Especially after being at SeaWorld, this roller coaster was extremely short, maybe two minutes at most, with a lot of tight turns that just banged you around. It wasn't thrilling at all compared to the roller coasters at SeaWorld.

We arrived around 7 PM and the park closes at 10 PM, but realistically we only had time to do that one ride. The rest of the time we were just walking around trying to see what else was open, and almost nothing was except for a few gift shops and food vendors. I was told the Safari is the best part of the park, but since it was already closed we missed that entirely.

I genuinely do not want to come back here, and I have never felt that way about an amusement park before. I am surprised they even let people in when they know you can have such a bad experience. I would not recommend going here.

I got free tickets to Busch Gardens, so I can't complain too much, but there are a few things worth noting. First, why is parking so expensive when tickets are already pricey? You have to drive to some random lot outside Tampa, they have tons of space in a grass lot, and I still had to pay $50 to park. Parking really makes or breaks an experience for me.

Then we got in line for two rides, waited around 45 minutes for each, and both were closed for maintenance. They recommended we stay in line, but never told us when the ride would be up and running again, which was frustrating and felt sketchy.

Busch Gardens is great for the first hour, but honestly it didn't feel that special after that. Maybe it's better during a festival because the rides are so spread apart. The animals weren't anything too impressive, though I did love the train ride and I'm a rollercoaster fan. Overall, the price doesn't really match what you get. Luckily I had free tickets, because paying over $100 a ticket for rides that constantly break down would be really disappointing.

My family and I had a great day as a courtesy from USAA and their family fun day! We were able to ride all the rides that we wanted to in the two hours that the park was closed to normal guests! All the employees were very nice and welcoming. Busch Gardens is always a fun time.

We grabbed food at Zagora Cafe, which was different for me. I usually eat at the Dragon Fire Grill & Pub. Although we had vouchers to use for food, the food prices are unfortunately pretty up there. My cousin, mom, and aunt all got the bacon cheeseburger and said they would not get it again. I got the chicken tenders because I personally find that easiest at amusement parks, and they were fine.

My gripe that I have with Busch Gardens and I feel this every time I go, is that there is no attendant near the front of the ride queue that directs your party to a lane. Busch Gardens is the only amusement park I've been to where it's like this. This honestly makes it very unorganized when it's busy. When you get to the front there's just a giant glob of people near the lanes and it makes it hard to decide which lane to go in. Then once your in the lanes you have to count the people in front of you so you're able to ride with your party (if it's larger than 2, and the ride is 2 to a row). I honestly feel that this adds to some of the wait times around the park. Every other amusement park that has an attendant to guide parties to lanes make it run so much smoother!

original_text: Plenty of parking on a Thursday. Buy tickets through my credit card, including the all day dining it was definitely worth it. One meal alone is $20 for one person. You can get an entrée drink and a dessert even aside but ice cream and treats pretzels popcorn is not included. Also bottled water is not part of the program. You can only get a soft drink. However, you can ask for a glass of water lines were at the most 20 minutes not all rides were available that day. impending storm can be thanked for that. All of my recent trips to Busch Gardens I have experienced low wait times and a lot of enjoyment. Being able to see this variety of animals, experience this level of entertainment and thrill rides at low wait times is excellent.

My one negative is the food prices, which are fairly high, so that is certaintly something that is important to note.

Iron Gwazi is worth the price of admission alone, it is the most intense rollercoaster in Florida, and is NOT for the faint of heart. But for the adrenaline junkies like me it's a must do every time!

original_text:
My family and I took advantage of the Waves of Honor program, which currently offers free admission to military veterans and their families. We chose to visit Busch Gardens on the Fourth of July, hoping for a fun day at the park and a great fireworks show.

We arrived just before 1 PM. Parking was $40 before tax, and we parked in a handicap-accessible section, which made the walk to the entrance fairly easy. We noticed consistent tram service for those parked farther away.

Once inside, I downloaded the Busch Gardens app to help us navigate. While it was helpful to have the map on hand, I really wish the map were interactive. It would've made it much easier to select dining locations, specific rides, or attractions and use a built-in GPS feature to guide us there directly. Instead, it's a flat map that leaves you trying to figure out how to navigate from section to section, which can be frustrating, especially in a large park.

Our first stop was the Sesame Street area, where my 4-year-old son enjoyed the canopy course for about 30 minutes. Afterward, we grabbed lunch at Zambi Smokehouse (I've posted a separate review for that).

Next, we visited the bird enclosure and fed the macaws ($10 per feeding cup). This was easily one of our favorite parts of the day. The birds perched on our arms and shoulders, and we got some great pictures.

We explored more animal exhibits, including the kangaroo walk-through, and made plans to return at 4:30 PM for a feeding session. Unfortunately, around 4 PM, a storm rolled in, and it rained until about 7 PM, cutting our day short. The rain delays also caused us to miss most of the scheduled shows and story times, which was another big disappointment.

Since we had traveled from South Florida, we used the storm break to check into our hotel. My son and I returned around 7 PM hoping to catch a few more rides and the 10 PM drone and fireworks show. However, we were told the Sesame Street area had lost power and wouldn't reopen. My son is only 42 inches tall, so most of the rides he could go on were in that section.

By 8 PM, power had been restored, but the rides still weren't running. When I asked an employee, she explained that no technicians were available to inspect the rides to make sure they would restart safely. While I absolutely understand the importance of safety, it was disappointing that they were so understaffed they couldn't prioritize this section of the park as much as the larger rides.

Around 8:45 PM, I definitely started to feel the mosquitoes. I really wish we had brought bug spray. We did have jackets, which helped protect our arms, but our legs weren't as lucky.

The crowd for the drone and fireworks show began gathering around 8 PM at the designated gates. I had a feeling it would be chaotic, so I chose to stay toward the back for easier exit access with the stroller. We were still experiencing on-and-off inclement weather, which made the large crowds and some impatient guests feel even more overwhelming and uncomfortable.

We had hoped to grab ice cream or a few quick snacks to enjoy while watching the fireworks, but most of the restaurants, snack stands, and even some gift shops were already closed by 9 PM. That was definitely frustrating, especially with the large number of guests still in the park and the biggest show of the night still to come.

The fireworks show itself was very short--15 minutes max. Many kids nearby expressed disappointment, saying it didn't feel worth the wait and they could have seen bigger fireworks at home. I found myself agreeing, it felt a bit underwhelming given the hype.

Exiting the venue was a mess. The crowds, stroller traffic, and bottlenecks made for a chaotic end to the night. But the worst part was the traffic leaving the parking lot. It took nearly an hour just to get from preferred and handicap parking to the main street exit. I truly believe this experience would be much smoother if park employees were stationed throughout the parking lot to direct traffic, which could reduce congestion and cut down on tensions between guests.

All in all, while I can't complain about a free ticket, and I understand the park has no control over the weather, this experience left a lot to be desired. I don't think I'll return for a few years. I'd rather wait until my son is older and taller, so he can fully enjoy more of what the park has to offer.

original_text:
It has been many years since I have been to Busch Gardens, so it was time for a refresher. Getting older has its perks...lol! We went with our kids, who are 11 and 8. Our family loves riding roller coasters, so if it's not for you, you may want to sit this one out.

Some of the favorite rides I went on are the Iron Gwazi, Phoenix Rising, Cheetah Hunt, Montu, Falcon's Fury, and the Skyride. The Kumba was temporarily closed :( Sometimes the wait was longer than others on some rides and it was worth the wait for the adrenaline rush it provided. We had a lot of fun and closed the park out in the rain!

There are animal exhibits and a safari the train takes customers through for sightseeing and a break off of your feet. The weather was hot and we stayed hydrated the entire day. The park provides water fountains for refillable bottles and I love it since I don't believe a lot of theme parks have it and expect people to pay a hefty price for a water bottle. Kudos to BG for rocking this perk out :D

The website offered a deal for one day admission tickets, so check there before you purchase them anywhere else. We purchased the all-day dining deal for $30 a person and was able to get an entree, side or dessert, and fountain drink every 90 minutes at select restaurants. You can easily spend at least $50 bucks for one meal alone for a family of four, so It's well worth the savings.

original_text:
Enjoyed a Saturday at Busch Gardens as well as the Atlanta Food and Wine festival. It's been at least 10 years since I've been here and the park has changed while also staying true to the African and family friendly things. Roller coasters are great, food options seem not particular diverse but a few bars for adults and a few themes depending on parts of the park. Kiddie rides, shows, and animal areas leave something for everything.

Food and wine festival was fun as well! You get to sample foods from over 15 countries and vendors as well as drinks and desserts. Loved France, Italy, and New Zealand. Live music was a nice touch as well from cover bands to the main acts.

I can't get over the cost and logistics for these parks! For a large family you're easily spending $1k to have a manageable day. Paying for parking, passes, lockers, food and more adds up quickly! The trick we found is to buy an annual pass which gives you a few free guest passes each year. You can also get a discount on parking or you can pay for premium parking. We then payed for the quick queue. Do not recommend going without it! Ride waits on Saturdays were easily 30-60 mins, with the pass we went straight to front. We also purchased the food and wine 18 punch ticket and spent about $650 all in. Whew!

Also check the weather! Usual Florida storms can cause multiple delays. Many passes for lockers, quick queue etc are for one day only. We went to guest relations, and they helped us to get another for next time.

original_text:
A list of pro/con

Tickets:
Positive: You can get Seaworld and Busch gardens both for same price as one alone. Or a fun pass for same as one day
Negative: Only worth it if you pay for quick queue so you don't spend the day in line
Negative: That quick queue is more than price of admission

Food
Positive: they have options even a Chick-fil-A. Positive: They offer meal ticket options.
Negative: it will cost a family of 4 $80+ to eat at Chick-fil-A and you will have to wait on long lines for the meal option restaurants if you go that route. Food quality and taste at other options hit or miss

Rides:
Positive: fun, fun, fun
Negative: only fun when running and open
Negative: that quick queue line can get lengthy at times when they don't have people to work or not running it as it should.
Negative: you will have to pay $4 for Iron Qwazi locker for the ride only to be closed. (I'm still salty here after all the other money I spent- if I can't have keys or phone on me supply the locker for free and I shouldn't have to pay. That's just wrong! )

Snacks and drinks:
Positives: you can go to most food/drink places and ask for cup of ice water and it's free
Negative: anything else is overpriced ($8 soda, $14 pretzel, etc)

Overall:
Pro:Kids are happy
Con: I'm broke and bitter over bad planning and management.

Choose wisely and best of luck! Make memories whether you end up here or not

original_text:
I had the chance to go to Busch Gardens and their Bier Fest on back to back weekends this year. We had lots of rain, but managed to have a good time anyway. The Bier Fest itself is actually in a small area within Busch Gardens. There are some cleverly named 'cabins' which are basically walled and roofed booths, Helga's Biergarten and The Springs Taproom that offer beers and unique foods. I was surprised at just how many craft brews were offered. I was more surprised to see no Anheuser Busch beer products for sale. I would have liked to have something other than a craft beer. The Bier Fest website does a good job of telling you what beers are available and where. There is also a display board in the Fest area for reference. Here's our opinion on each that we tried.
Helga's Biergarten had the only live entertainment with a band playing German music, demonstrating the long Alpenhorn, mens and ladies stein hoisting contests and more. Salted Mini Salted Pretzel Bites With Beer Cheese Dipping Sauce. Not worth the money. Beer Braised Currywurst and Sauerkraut with Tater Tots. This was good. A full size sausage. Bratwurst with Onion Mustard Relish with Tater Tots. This was good. Also a full size sausage. CABIN 2: Tipsy Bird. Smoked Pulled Duck Roll. Pulled duck salad with a brioche roll with grape apple slaw and house pickles. Very good with plenty of duck. CABIN 3: Island Hoppers. Mango Jerk Chicken Wings. Tender chicken wings in spicy jerk seasoning and grilled with sweet mango for a smoky, sweet island kick and fluffy coconut rice. Only 1 wing and 1 drummy on a scoop of rice. Not a good jerk flavor, not worth it. Jamaican Surf & Turf (Shrimp & Beef Skewers). Garlic-lime shrimp and seasoned beef skewers with coconut rice. Skip it. 2 very small pieces of beef on the sides of one skewered small shrimp. CABIN 4: Mis Amigos Cabin. Churrasco Street Taco. Marinated grilled steak, sliced thin and served on a warm corn tortilla with chopped onions and fresh cilantro. Two tacos that were very good and good value. Charred Street Corn. Sweet corn grilled over open flame and slathered in chili-lime mayo, cotija cheese, and a hint of lime. Tasty. The corn was grilled, but this is served as loose kernels in a cup. We liked it. CABIN 6: Local Knowledge. BBQ Grilled Pinchos (Beef Tips & Shishito). Marinated beef and pepper kabobs with sweet plantain mash. Pretty good. Shishito is not hot. CABIN 7: Root Beer Floats. Peanut Butter Stout Float. Stout with peanut butter syrup and vanilla ice cream. This was good. CABIN 9: Out of the Box. Brisket Mac (in a Waffle Cone). Mac and cheese topped with smoked brisket in a waffle cone. I think our most favorite item. Plenty of brisket in creamy cheesy mac. Mini Bison Slider with Honey Chipotle Slaw. Bison slider with chipotle-honey slaw. Forget it. Very small burger and dry as shoe leather. Grilled Stuffed Avocado. Grilled avocado with brisket, roasted corn, and cilantro cream. Served cold. Flavorless. Pass on this. Chocolate Pecan Tart. Dark chocolate ganache tart with toasted pecans and sea salt. Excellent dessert. Very rich. 3 people can share. CABIN 10: Lost in the Sauce. Root Beer Ribs. Fall Off the Bone Root Beer Braise St. Louis Ribs, Root Beer BBQ Sauce, and Jalapeño cornbread. They give you 3 ribs. Sticky with good flavor. Southern Peach Sonker (Peach Cobbler). Funnel cake peach cobbler with whipped cream. Tied with the Pecan Tart for best dessert. Get it, you'll love it. The Springs Taproom. A regular building with great A/C to escape the heat and tables to sit at. They did have a full bar with many craft beers on tap. Bier Fest punch cards were accepted here. When it comes to getting the best value, buy the Sampler Punch Cards. Either 5 ($40) or 8 ($65) items per card so the cost per sampled item is $8.00. That is much better than paying the prices on the menu boards at the different cabins. We saw some food items priced at $13+. As for beer, one punch per 10 ounce beer. I did purchase a 20 ounce oatmeal stout at the Springs Taproom and I think it was $17.00. Oh well. You can buy the cards right at the park.
As for the rest of the park, it's small enough to get around and see a lot in one day. During one of the downpours, we went to the Rhythm of Nature ice skating show. A good show, included in park admission, and fun to see real ice in Florida. We also took a ride on the Serengeti Express Train which winds through the property and through their Serengeti Plain, loaded with all kinds of animals. I know I've never been closer to rhinos before. There's a tour guide telling riders what they're seeing and some interesting info about some. This is also no extra charge. It is a zoo with rides so there are many cool looking coasters and natural areas with a variety of animals. We found the swimming penguins fun to watch. I would go back as I would like to try a few of the more tame rides. A fun place and a fun event.

original_text:
I had some lingering BG commercials in my mind from the 80s or something, but this theme park is not somewhere you have to visit.

The tickets were over $100 -- they look cheaper online, but then the price rises depending on when you visit. Parking was $40 - 60! OMG. The park was partially closed (no mention of that online), not all the animals were available (not mentioned). Even with that situation, they still upcharge for so many things, like the rickety gondola ride over the park.

This park has some wild rollercoasters, so if you were a real enthusiast, then it might be worthwhile. However, if you're looking for kind of thrilling rides, some fun rides, and other things to do -- it's so limited.

The rides demand that you lock up your belongings. Each locker is $4. And, they have surprise overtime charges if you don't close up your rental when your 2 hours expires. It's like that throughout the park -- nickel and diming you for everything. Someone was like - "So it's like Disney'' and no -- because Disney is worth it and really nice. This is like a sad, broken-down theme park.

The food -- grooooooosssssss. Our tickets came with a "meal." It's really just an entree. If you get the all-day meal-deal, they let you get an entree, drink, and dessert (for a meal). But, again, this cheap way of getting you a ticket + meal (which is how ours was advertised) was just an entree. The entrees are so unhealthy and the food quality is so poor, but the prices are like $18 for chicken tenders or $19 for a personal pizza. The cups have codes on them, so you don't DARE get a refill.

Everything is monetized here. If you want to dry off in these fun dryers after the Congo River -- you have to pay. If you want to spray other riders going through the Congo River -- you have to pay! Pay, pay, pay, pay. It's really bad and very obvious. The people who work there were nice, they often didn't know what was going on. Even the cashier at the food court didn't know how the meal included in your ticket works, but she was nice enough to figure it out. No one is really managing the rides.

You can just imagine some horrible little man at the top of the Busch Garden franchise twiddling his fingers and thinking how he can squeeze more money out of the people. It's gross. I think I spent $300 for my son and me and it was a mediocre experience, where we'll never return. Just glad I didn't have my whole family with us!

original_text:
TLDR: this park is going downhill and we witnessed it firsthand. Save your time and your money.

My husband and I traveled from Nebraska and were excited to visit this park. The coasters and zoo concept are great, but the management and staff made the experience incredibly disappointing.

From the moment we entered, employees seemed untrained and uninterested. No one could explain our ticket bundle--we were sent back and forth between ride attendants and guest relations, given only a printed barcode, and constantly met with eye rolls, sighs, and confusion. Even basic things like scanning passes or knowing that mobile tickets work seemed unknown to staff.

Ride rules were inconsistent: we were told to use paid lockers, only to see others carry large bags on. Then our locker wouldn't open, and every employee we asked passed us off to someone else. We waited 30-40 minutes for help while staff laughed at us instead of assisting. The only helpful person we met all day was Ann, who finally resolved the locker issue.

Gluten-free awareness was also terrible--staff seemed completely uninformed about options or safety, and I did consume gluten while there.

We lost out on multiple experiences, including all Christmas festivities, because of the locker issue taking up our last hour of an already shortened day . We even tried to speak with guest relations again, but the line was too long before our shuttle departed due to the rain delays.

Overall, this was the worst-managed park we've visited, and it significantly affected our day. Spend the extra money and go to Universal or Disney, at least you get what you pay for

Don't bother using customer service. That's just as terrible. No personalized reply or 'care' given.

original_text:
This statement is submitted for the permanent record.

On June 19, 2025, my family and I visited the park after traveling from Las Vegas, Nevada, under the conventional--though apparently outdated--assumption that payment is exchanged for services rendered. Upon arrival, we were assisted by a ticket window representative who actively and persuasively pushed the purchase of Quick Queue passes, advising that this premium upgrade was necessary to meaningfully experience the park. We were further informed that this upgrade required the purchase of standard admission tickets as well. Trusting the park's own representation of its operations, we complied.

The total expenditure exceeded $600.

What followed was an impressively efficient demonstration of transactional finality.

Shortly after entering the park, attractions began shutting down in sequence. Rides closed. Animal interactions closed. The practical utility of Quick Queue access--having fulfilled its purpose at the point of sale--became purely theoretical. Within approximately 30 minutes, a public announcement instructed all guests to exit the park due to weather-related operational shutdown. The experience concluded promptly, decisively, and without ambiguity.

At that moment, the park was no longer providing the product for which payment had been accepted.

The ticket terms, of course, reference "Non-Refundable, Non-Transferable, and Non-Replaceable." This language is admirably clear. It establishes, with refreshing candor, that once payment is processed, performance is optional. In application, it permits the collection of over $600 in exchange for three rides and a mandatory evacuation. From a purely mechanical standpoint, this is a remarkably streamlined model.

We did not immediately request a refund, operating in good faith and under the mistaken belief that a park selling premium access might also possess a functional contingency plan. When it became evident that operations would not resume and guests were being dismissed, we inquired about accommodations. None were offered. No refund. No partial credit. No future admission. No voucher. Just a polite apology--delivered, one assumes, as a symbolic gesture intended to substitute for value.

Charging premium pricing for expedited access immediately prior to rendering that access unusable--particularly after a staff member actively upsold it--reflects a business strategy in which risk is aggressively monetized and entirely externalized. Responsibility, meanwhile, remains safely abstract. The apology, while professionally delivered, did not materially improve the return on investment.

For those inclined to view this as an isolated incident, a brief review of publicly available feedback suggests otherwise. The park's aggregate rating of approximately 3.2 speaks for itself. Patterns emerge quickly: operational shutdowns, rigid policies, and an absence of meaningful guest accommodation. This experience does not appear anomalous; it appears representative.

This incident illustrates a disciplined adherence to fine print over fairness, and a notable willingness to sell premium products regardless of imminent operational risk. It also raises unavoidable questions regarding upselling ethics, disclosure timing, and whether guest equity is considered at any point beyond the transaction itself. While this approach may technically comply with internal policy, it does so at the expense of trust, credibility, and long-term brand integrity.

I will not return to this park, nor will I patronize any affiliated or subsidiary properties. Prospective guests should understand that premium upgrades may be actively promoted moments before becoming unusable, and that operational shutdowns are treated as a customer inconvenience rather than a business responsibility.

This record is submitted so expectations may be calibrated accurately--for future guests, and for those at the executive level tasked with determining whether policy compliance alone constitutes success.

This was the letter I sent today after our visit.

To whom it may concern,

My name is David. My wife, baby, mother, father and 2 friends went to your park at Busch gardens Tampa Bay today, November 3, 2024.

Today, we drove 3 plus hours to go to your park. Including the park tickets, we paid 35 dollars for parking and bought 2 all day meals for 67 each. So we invested a ton of money into today to have a great friend and family day especially for my 16 month old daughters first theme park experience.

When we entered the park, no one said anything about most of the rides being closed or said anything on social media about things being closed, much less most of your roller coasters being closed. A couple finally opened later in the day by around 1 pm. We rode a total of 1 ride today which was the carousel. We tried riding the Congo river ride which was closed. All your coasters except for 2 were closed. A third one as previously mentioned open around 1pm. Of course, we didn't ride any rides due to the long lines.

More of your attractions were closed including the elephants. We learned your train was closed for yearly maintenance as well as something else.

original_text:
I absolutely love Busch Gardens. The Animal Kingdom for big kids, I like to call it.

I have been here a couple of times now, and it truly is a wonderland for any theme park enthusiast. While I tend to stick with the Orlando parks while in the area, I would not hesitate to drive a couple hours over to Tampa to come here.

When it comes to its theming, BG does it so well. The whole park resembles a jungle feel and will make you think you have transported into one. There are also animals inside the park, so it also is like a zoo hybrid.

What you come here for are their coasters. They have one of the best collections of them that I have seen in one park. Some personal favorites are Sheikra, Iron Gwazi and Cheetah Hunt!

They have some pretty neat events too - We came during the food and wine festival, and it was just like Epcot getting to go around and try different food and drinks!

Busch Gardens will always have my heart. As soon as I leave, I anticipate my next visit!

4 stars because the dining options are limited for those who are Gluten Free.

They have Pizza in a bag & it comes with its own pizza cutter. It costs $25 for a small personal pizza. I personally did not get it, we had dinner reservations elsewhere.

They do have a chick fil a, however it was closed on Sunday - the day I went.

The park was rather empty. I'm unsure if it's due to the recession that's approaching, because they have way less rides than another amusement park (such as six flags), or if everyone is flocking to the new universal epic theme park. For it being middle / late May, on a Sunday... I expected more of a crowd.

We upgraded to the fast pass & completed all the rides for adults by 3pm.

We watched the ice skating show, it was only 20 mins but it had some incredibly talented skaters.

original_text:
First time visiting Busch Garden in Tampa. I remember taking my son to the one in Williamsburg.
When he was younger. I've never been much on fearless roller-coasters. I'm a pretty mellow kind of gal. I love the milder coasters. My favorite is actually the animals. Busch Garden has a lot of animals.

We caught the train, and we were able to see so many different animals. It was so exciting. Even though the train ride was only twenty minutes. You could see so much in that time. They also gave you a description of each animal.

It's definitely worth it. It's also included free as part of the park. We purchased a one day pass. They had a special going on for the 4th of July. We went on a Monday. Which was perfect because it wasn't too crowded.
The only thing is that we paid additional for the skyride. It was an additional $5 per person, which wasn't bad at all. I love sky riding. The views are amazing. You see other parts of the park that you might have missed.

We also caught a lot of shows. I really enjoyed all of them, including the ice skating show, which is relatively new.
The day was scorching hot, so the water ride was very refreshing. You will definitely get soaked. They offer general parking where you would get a trolley train because of the distance.

We decided to do the premium parking. Which was walking distance. We also purchased the all-day dining.
It was definitely worth it. Just make sure you only choose fountain drinks. It does not include bottled water.

You get one main course, one side or dessert, and one fountain drink every 90 minutes. I didn't like that they didn't offer a bracelet. If you had a printed out sheet. Anyone could use it. I think they should offer bracelets.

Other than that, wear your comfortable shoes because you will get your steps in. We really had a good time!!

Nostalgia, thrill, excitement. This is what Busch Gardens means to me.

Growing up in FL for many years we would always go to Busch Gardens. Thanks to my parents, I learned to love roller coasters and be fearless. This place brought the love for animals and thrill rides to one happy place.

Fast forward to years later, being back at this incredible place where I used to ride classics like kumba, montu, Congo River rapids, it's like nothing has changed. Except, I don't have to worry about being tall enough for the rides hehe.

Got the craziest deal for Black Friday where for the same price of a one day ticket we got fun passes, good enough for the whole year.

The same exciting rides are there with so many incredible add ons like sheikra and probably my all time favorite, gwazi. If you haven't been to a roller coaster park in a while it can feel a little out of place. But honestly, it's just like riding a bike. Just get on, let go, and ride.

I'm a sucker for amusement parks and rides and all the fun stuff but Busch Gardens will always and forever be the top rollercoaster park for me.

original_text:
As a Florida native who frequented Busch Gardens for many years as a child/teenager and is now visiting as an adult, there has been a decrease in the shine and quality of the park compared to how it was years ago. I feel this park is truly only worth going to if you're a coaster lover; if you're an annual pass holder (i.e., able to come more than once in a year's time because you're not far away); and/or you love seeing the animals.

They have some of the best variety of rollercoasters in the park, which is a major draw. If you do get an annual pass, it does end up being worth it for the free guest tickets you get and bonuses (like Busch Bucks to spend on food and drinks, discounted tickets to special events, free parking, etc.). They are doing a better job of incorporating local elements, for example the Bier Fest that features local craft beer.

However, there are a lot of things overlooked that seem to be rooted in poor practices and short staffing the park. Many times, we've had low quality food for the astronomical price you pay. Paying $30-$40 for a one time parking fee is ridiculous. We once wanted to enjoy the patio outside the Giraffe Bar, but the tables didn't look like they'd been cleaned in days. There was sticky food, crumbs, and flies everywhere. They've had health violations at their restaurants. It's just not a good look overall, especially when the whole goal is to be outside enjoying the space. Most people we saw ended up leaving the area because of this. During Summer Nights, they were often still shutting down restaurants, vending carts, and even some rides--as though the park were going to close at a regular time. We've tried to use self-service kiosks around the park, only to find out they're shut down. At times we've gone to refill the drink cup you can buy to refill during your visits, only for the machines to have no ice on a hot summer day (who wants a warm Coke?!)

These are just a few examples of some disappointments we had in a few short visits. It's such a shame because I remember how vibrant, fun, and seemingly well-kept the park was years ago. Short staffing the park and not paying people well is absolutely not working in their favor. We got a gift ticket that we were able to upgrade to an annual pass, but this will be our last time doing it if we don't see some major improvements.

Long post incoming, summary: overpriced, many rides closed, many food/snack places closed, and the food that was open was not great.

Yesterday my husband and I went to Busch Gardens. We were looking forward to our visit. We had QuickPass bands which included the eat all day pass. We were only able to ride two rides during our time there. The wait time was almost nothing for both rides, even without the QuickPass. Many rides were closed, I would say I saw a total of 5 rides open throughout the day. We were going to ride the sky ride but that was closed in the beginning of the day and when it opened it was an extra cost. Same thing with the safari.
The food plan was okay, except there were about 4 places it could actually be used at. One of which was the worst meal I ever had. Starting off, there were 4 sections of food that you could choose from: Italian, Mexican, American, and Asian. I went with Mexican because the line was the shortest to be honest. When we got toward the front of the line (after an insanely long wait because the food server walked away without an explanation) I noticed that the server was not wearing gloves. He just opened a pack of tortillas and put some on a person's plate with his bare hands. Disgusting and unsanitary. I asked him to put gloves on and he did. I will note that everyone else I saw working in the kitchen or serving the line did have gloves on. I then ordered a chicken bowl. He proceeded to give me beef which I don't eat, saying that it was chicken. He obviously had the TWO options mixed up. The rice in the bowl was rock hard, and the entire dish was incredibly salty.
We did have a better food experience with the pizza shop near the Jaguar ride. Mid-tier food but the servers were polite (and clean!).
Overall not a good experience and we will not be back.

original_text:
On March 2nd, 2026 (yesterday) my wheel chair bound son and I visited Busch Gardens Tampa Bay with our recently purchased annual passes (this was only our second visit). What should have been a fun day quickly turned into one of the most humiliating experiences I've ever had as a parent.

After already riding the Skyride earlier in the day without issue, we went to my son's favorite attraction, Congo River Rapids. When attempting to use the accessible entrance, we were stopped by staff members who demanded "proof" that my son was actually handicapped. I gestured to the wheelchair I was pushing and asked if that wasn't sufficient. I was told, confidently, that it was not and that they "didn't know what he has."

We were then told we needed to complete paperwork detailing his exact disability before he could ride. After complying and filling out the requested documentation, we were still denied access and made to wait while a supervisor was called. During this process, we felt detained, embarrassed, and singled out in front of other guests.

Once we were finally permitted to board, staff members--including individuals identified as Kaylee and her supervisor Alex at Congo River Rapids--were laughing while my son and I were preparing to get him safely onto the ride. Regardless of the intent, it felt incredibly disrespectful and demeaning in an already uncomfortable situation.

It is important to note that there was no "advantage" being gained by using the accessible entrance. Due to the Strawberry Festival, the park was not busy and there were no significant wait times.

We later visited Guest Services to express our concerns and request a refund for our annual passes, which were less than a month old. The supervisor there, identified as Bri, stated that refunds would not be considered once passes were used and offered a complimentary lunch item for a future visit instead. Given the experience we had just endured, returning was not something we were interested in.

Families with special needs children should not have to justify or publicly explain their child's medical condition to enjoy a day at a theme park. What happened to us was unnecessary, humiliating, and handled without compassion.

I hope this is addressed so no other family has to experience what we did.

original_text:
**Second Visit to Tampa Bay Busch Gardens in 2024/7: A roller coaster lover's Paradise**

For anyone who loves thrilling roller coasters, Busch Gardens in Tampa Bay is a must-visit. We decided to add it again to our WDW's itinerary, especially since summer tickets are half-off at just $65 per person. Plus, there's an end-of-summer sale running until September 27, 2024, where you can get two tickets for $111.99.

Busch Gardens and SeaWorld are part of the same company, so they offer a free shuttle bus service from Orlando to Tampa Bay, with stops at six locations. The one-way trip takes about an hour and a half. You can book your ride at https://buschgardens.mears.com/

During the summer, Busch Gardens stays open until 9 PM, whereas in January, it was closed at 6 PM. The summer crowds are noticeably larger, with many young visitors. On our trip, two full buses were needed just to accommodate everyone departing from Orlando.

We opted for the dining plan this time, which costs $59.99 per person for the day. It allowed us to get a meal or snacks every 90 minute. For us, one meal plan was enough for two adults, making it a great deal. If you're bringing kids aged 3-8, their plan is $29.99.

**Our Day in 2024/7 at Busch Gardens**

- We boarded the shuttle at the Publix on Vineland Avenue at 9:19 AM and arrived Bush Gardens at 10:50 AM.
- 11:05 AM: We headed straight to the most popular ride, *Iron Gwazi* (65-minute wait).
- Afterward, we grabbed our first meal at *Sheikra Eats*: the *Tender Tower*, and took a break.
- 12:28 PM: Rode *Sheikra* (20-minute wait).
- 1:21 PM: Headed to *Tigris* (20-minute wait but didn't board until 2:05 PM).
- *Jungala*: Park provided free beer promotion (2 small cups per person).

The heat really slowed us down, and with the larger summer crowds, the wait times were longer, so we decided to grab another meal.

- 2:23 PM: Picked up a Chick-fil-A meal--great value with a sandwich, fries, and a drink (normally costs $11).
- After lunch, we rested for two hours at *Tiger Lodge* (It is the perfect spot to see tigers and enjoy air conditioning). Once we were recharged, it was time for another meal.
- 4:27 PM: Another Chick-fil-A meal.
- 5:10 PM: Rode *Kumba* (boarded at 5:55 PM).
- 6:03 PM: Grabbed one more Chick-fil-A meal to-go.

The free shuttle was set to leave at 7:15 PM, so we missed out the evening fireworks (9:00-9:30 PM).

**Second Visit Insights**
-- Most waiting areas for rides at Busch Gardens are shaded with large fans, making the wait less uncomfortable compared to Disney. Tampa Bay's coastal weather is definitely cooler than inland Orlando.

-- The park isn't huge, so you can easily cover it in a day.

-- The dining plan is a good value--one plan is enough for two people.

-- Popular rides often experience temporary closures, so keep an eye on the app for updates.

-- Wait times on the app aren't as accurate as Disney's.

-- You need to store your bags in lockers before riding each attraction, which is a bit inconvenient (Day locker rental is $10).

-- We stopped at Publix on our way back and found four aisles of Disney merchandise at lower prices than in the parks--perfect for gifts. The Publix is right next to Orlando Vineland Premium Outlets, so if you're staying nearby and having time, it's worth a visit for some great deals.

original_text:
After over five years is passholder I am canceling my annual pass. Things have really gone down hill in recent years. We used to love Busch Gardens because it was a great place to bring my son and we could spend the day in Sesame Street land or in pantopia riding kid rides but no more. In in late 2023, they ripped out all of the kid rides in Pantopia to make way for yet another roller coaster. So we lost half the rides that my child could do. This included motorcycles, a small train, small airplanes, a pirate ship and a small roller coaster. Busch gardens has made no effort to replace all the missing rides for small children.
Additionally, they always seem to be short staffed. Sesame Street safari of fun is the perfect example. Two weekends in a row we've come, and the Grover roller coaster has been broken. They only have one person running both the kitty log flume and the snuffy elephant ride. They only have one person running big bird eggs, and the swings. My child has recently outgrown the swings and so we're trying to wait for the big bird eggs and a cast member was rude and told us it would be over an hour before she would be able to open up the big bird eggs. Lately we've come and only been able to do the count von count cars and the playground in Sesame Street land because the other rides were not operating. So if your kid is under 48 inches or too scared to do the big roller coasters there's really not much for you to do here.
Busch Gardens is not remotely size inclusive. None of the new rides fit larger size bodies. These are rides that have been built in recent years, and they could've made a better effort to include more people.
The food is very expensive and not that good. We have a popcorn bucket for literally every Theme Park in Florida because it is my son's favorite snack. Instead of a $2 refill like Disney, the refill here is $5. Last time we got one it was so salty, it was almost inedible.
I'm really sad because we used to LOVE to come here, but there really isn't much for us to do here anymore.

original_text:
My friend and I flew down to ride coasters for 2 days and over all it was a fine experience.

There's a lot of stuff in this park that needs improvements though.

First and foremost - customer service. Every single person there wasn't polite and didn't help us when we asked for it. Most places when there are problems try to resolve your issues. They did nothing. We purchased a photo pass for one day which is $53 which is a complete rip off. We bought it on the app and there were no instructions on how to use it. So we rode a bunch of rides and then no one was working at the photo kiosks after the rides so we didn't know what to do. When we finally approached the main kiosk at the front of the park they told us how the photo stuff works and we didn't get any of our photos. And we didn't get a refund. UNACCEPTABLE. They did absolutely zero to help right the situation and every excuse was "this is a new system" or "it's my first day".

Another problem and rip off is the Christmas town meal stuff. First off the park is basically throwing up Christmas every where but their "Christmas Town" was this tiny area with food stalls and if you did purchase the food samples it's another $50 and not worth it from what we could see. No one was informative about anything.

The next thing: water. Literally every other amusement park I've been to will give you a free cup of water but Busch gardens doesn't and it's any where from $5-7 per bottle. Rip. OFF. They do have water filling station and by that I mean ONE. At the back of the park.
Overall we had a good day riding the rides but overall this park is severely lacking and for the same amount of money you can go literally any where else.

As long as I have lived in Florida and in the area, this was my first time coming to Busch Gardens Tampa Bay. I was beyond excited because it was a day full of just roller coasters and animals, two of my favorite things. I will say seeing some of the animals is pretty sad because of the conditions they are in, but overall it is interesting seeing the different types of animals.

When thinking of Busch Gardens, I think of roller coasters. In comparison to its local competition, Disney and Universal, Busch Gardens is packed full of high speed, intense, roller coasters. It is made more for older kids and adults due to its nature of its structure. With that being said there is a small number of kids coasters and an area of kids to hangout or play. I would recommend taking any kids under the height tallest requirement to a different park due to this.

Over the course of the park, the rides continued to improve. We tried various rides and even rode some twice. Some of my personal favorites included the iron Gwazi, Tigris, and Shakri.

original_text:
We have now visited all of the SeaWorld and Busch Gardens park now and spent 2 full days here. One on a Sunday, when it was more busy and the other on a Tuesday when less busy, but less hours to have fun.

If you go on the weekends, you are getting more shows and more food options to be opened especially if you plan on buying the all dining pass. I would recommend getting the pass on the weekends as on a less busy day, the food passes that are not as good are closed.

Layout can be confusing and some of the paths can be hidden as I tend to get lost some of the times, but I followed the signs to get where I was at. I did like the themes, the Egyptian, Africa and Morocco. The theming is pretty good, but it is not as beautiful as Williamsburg, but I prefer this park. I would suggest riding the Serengeti Train one time, however, they do take forever to load and you might need to wait 25 minutes for them to depart. But you get to see the animals without doing the extra charge of going on the safari car. The park is huge and prepare to walk.

We did not see any shows as they were not showing on the weekend and focus more of the rides and seeing the animals.

We rode the following rides and my quick thoughts on these as I did a full review on these. Sheikra, Skyride and Congo River Rapids were closed which I enjoy riding on.

1. Iron Gwazi - you might need to wait 30 minutes before this ride opens and on the weekday they were having technical difficulties so we waited till 2pm to start riding
2. Cheetah Hunt - this is a popular ride
3. Montu - they only run 1 train
4. Cobra's Curse - long line on the weekday
5. Falcon's Fury - must do, but do not do this if you are scared of heights
6. Tigiris
7. Stanley Flume Falls - you do get soaked on there
8. Scorpion - line moves slow, but ride once if you need the credit
9. Kumba - this shakes quite a bit
10. Train Station - the scenery is cool and seeing the animals, but the wait might be long

I did take a look at the Sesame Street, but the place was small, but they do have a small showing area. It looked nice of the kids to play on.

Food - we ate at the following, please note that some of the places do not take the dining plan and close 1 hour before the park closes
1. Zambia Smokehouse - must go to, we loved the ribs
2. Chick Fila - beware of the birds while eating outside, they will follow you and if you drop the food they will be aggressive
3. Moroccan Delights - they serve burgers, chicken strips - not too bad if you want a bit to eat
4. Sergenti Cafe - We ate the pizza and was not too bad. I think at that point I was getting full.

Overall, I had a great experience and would definitely come here again for 2 days.

original_text:
Busch Gardens Tampa is a park that frustrates me. I took my first and only visit to the park in 2020 and I was NOT disappointed. The operations were stellar, the staff were awesome, the park was clean and well themed, the animal exhibits were amazing, it was just an outstanding park! Since then, they've added some new stuff like Iron Gwazi and Phoenix Rising which does make me excited to return, however, they've also been taking away a lot of that stuff (big rant on that down below).

Now, when I went, the park's star attraction with out a doubt was Montu. At the time, Montu was my favorite roller coaster of all time. It's dropped off considerably since then because, I've ridden better coasters. But Montu still ranks among my top B&Ms and is easily one of the best inverts in the world (only behind Banshee at Kings Island). After that, there's Kumba. This is famous for being one of the first B&Ms to ever open, and it's a good one. Like Montu, this is also a really strong coaster being super intense despite it being on the rough side. Unfortunately, this has been closed for the past year and is on the chopping block for removal. I'm so mad about that I can hardly put it into words. There was also Scorpion, an short but awesome Schwartzkopf looper, which was torn down last year with only a few days' notice. Which again, holy sh*t. United Parks (and ESPECIALLY SIX FLAGS) needs to F*CKING ANNOUNCE THIS AHEAD OF TIME. Sure, Scorpion and Kumba have service lives that are/were probably up. But LOOK AT WHAT UNIVERSAL DID TO RIP RIDE ROCKIT. ONE OF THE SH*TTIEST COASTERS I'VE EVER RIDDEN. THAT CLOSURE WAS ANNOUNCED A YEAR BEFORE IT HAPPENED, AND IT GENERATED 3-HOUR LONG WAITS ON ITS CLOSING DAY. WHY COULD THIS NOT BE DONE FOR SCORPION, A RIDE FAR BETTER AND MORE ICONIC!??!?!?!? So infuriating. Anyways, besides those three, they have Tigris, a Premier Skyrocket II, those are always fun, Cheetah Hunt and Cobra's Curse which are great family coasters. So yeah, they still have a very strong coaster collection, especially with Iron Gwazi now open (really looking forward to that on my next visit).

For their other rides, they have Falcon's Fury, one of the biggest drop towers in the world. Which has a TILTING feature that makes it so when you drop, you stare right at the ground. Easily my favorite drop tower and favorite flat ride. They also have a rapids ride, and they used to have a log ride (Also demolished with hardly any notice. Yeah, it wasn't the best log flume, but it was still a park classic that deserved better.) So yeah, it's mostly just coasters with a few other notable things like the train, the skyride (which, for some reason, is now an upcharge ride, PLEASE fix this). I'm also really concerned for the future of this park as it's been on a decline as of late. Constantly demolishing rides, having multiple rides closed for MONTHS at a time, and I've heard several stories of the operations and quality getting worse and worse. I mean, good god, they had to shut down a restaurant mid-operations due to a f*cking failed health inspection. F*ck that noise!

So yeah, this park sadly lost a spot in my top 10 parks list. But Montu and Falcon's Fury still keep it in the upper half of my park rankings (So help me if they ever decide to get rid of those two rides). So I'd still recommend checking it out. I just hope that the park hasn't gotten as bad as I've heard. ):

Where do I begin?

$35 for parking...insane, but don't waste your money on premium parking ($60). I think those fools walked farther than is.

Wait lines for rides are terrible. It's hot, muggy, and loud. If you want to skip those line ($120 a person). If this was Disney - maybe, but the rides don't warrant that amount of money.

Food is mediocre at best, and that's if you get it. We ate at "Tree Top Kitchen". It's good to escape the heat, but the meal was terrible and service slow to nonexistent. Once my family's order did come, my meal was still missing. I brought it to the host and servers attention multiple times, but they couldn't, or wouldn't help me?

I don't recommend this park to the worst person in the world.

Spend the extra money to go to Disney or Universal.

original_text:
Every December, I bring my family to Florida for two weeks to celebrate Christmas, New Year's, and my son's birthday. This year, at my son's request, we visited SeaWorld and Busch Gardens.

We visited SeaWorld on December 24, 2025, and had a very positive experience. On December 26, 2025, we visited Busch Gardens. For most of the day, our experience was enjoyable. However, as the evening progressed--around 5:00 PM--we noticed the crowd becoming increasingly large, aggressive, and hostile. By approximately 7:00 PM, my wife and I decided it was best to leave the park due to these conditions.

While walking toward the exit near the penguin exhibit, a group of approximately three to four women were taking photographs. As we walked past, one woman stepped backward, causing her to bump into my wife. The woman began yelling at my wife, using profanity. My wife responded that the woman had stepped into her path and continued walking away. As I passed, I calmly stated that the woman had stepped back into my wife and asked her to calm down.

At that point, the woman became increasingly aggressive, verbally threatening and advancing toward me while I was holding my son's hand. Despite repeated attempts to disengage and walk away, the woman continued to scream, invade our personal space, and make threatening gestures. My wife urged us to leave, but the woman persisted.

Moments later, the woman struck me in the face. Fearing for the safety of my family, I felt forced to defend myself. This escalated into a physical altercation in which two to three individuals from the same group attacked both my wife and me. Other guests intervened and separated us, and we attempted to leave the area.

However, the same individual repeatedly broke free from others restraining her and attacked us again--this occurred multiple times as we attempted to walk away. At no point during these incidents did security personnel or park staff intervene.

After the final altercation, my wife realized her phone and earrings had been stolen during the assault. Using a phone tracking application, we located the group again, where the phone was being hidden in a baby stroller. When my wife identified the phone, one of the individuals attempted to destroy it by throwing it. When I bent down to retrieve the phone, I was struck again.

We were eventually able to make our way toward the park exit, still without assistance from Busch Gardens security or staff. My wife was forced to call 911 while we were walking so that police could meet us at the front gate, as we continued to fear being followed and attacked.

As a result of this incident, I sustained a severe shoulder injury, multiple bruises on my back, and a black eye. My wife also sustained multiple bruises and a black eye. All of this occurred in front of our children, which was extremely traumatic.

The responding police officer and medical personnel were professional and helpful. However, no arrests were made and no charges were filed. We were then informed that we would be trespassed from Busch Gardens properties for 365 days--despite being the victims and receiving no protection from park security during the incident.

This decision is deeply troubling. My family visited your park to create positive family memories, yet we experienced a violent assault without any intervention from your security team. We were forced to defend ourselves due to the absence of assistance, suffered serious injuries, and then were penalized by being trespassed from your properties.

Earlier this morning, I was contacted by one of your local attorneys and was advised to seek medical attention immediately. I intend to follow this advice upon returning home and will be seeing my primary care physician.

In closing, while we appreciate the opportunity to visit your park, Busch Gardens failed to provide a safe and secure environment for my family. The lack of security response, combined with the decision to trespass the victims rather than the aggressors, is unacceptable. Due to this experience, we will most likely never patronize any Busch Gardens or affiliated facilities again.

Sincerely,
Vernard M

Updating my review to improve the score because Busch Gardens outdid themselves with the 2023 annual pass members summer party they had for those who visited 20 times this summer.

Whoever put this party together should be commended for far exceeding what was expected for Busch Gardens, especially lately. Almost everything they did that night was magical. From the champagne, including mocktails, as you entered, to allowing us to pick from whatever fancy drinks we wanted (times two), to snacks (x2) and even one free play on the fair games where every single person could have won a stuffed animal. No lines for the four coasters they had open this Special night! And then to blow our minds by handing out $35 huge beach towels AND a gourmet cupcake to go! OMG

Being an annual pass holder this summer really meant something!

They also recently had a special day in July where they allowed us to tour one of the haunted houses with a couple surprises at the end AND at the Giraffe Bar had Santa Clause available to visit.

They are building a new coaster in Pantopia where Sand Serpent and a couple kids rides were located, so excited to see what that will be since they've already cleared the land but made no announcement yet.

Thanks for improving Tampa's #1 playground destination and making it more fun again!

Now if you could just get the sky ride open after being down the past five years, that would take the rating to five stars.

Doing tourist things this was on the list of to-do's on our family vacation to the Tampa/Bradenton beach area.....Great place to see all the different things Busch Gardens had to offer. This park was a great time for us as a family.
The food stations/vendors had a variety of menus and were sporadic throughout the park. and pricing was pretty expensive considering the quality of foods....we did the all day dining option.... probably would do again.... The BBQ place had a staffing issue that caused a HUGE delay. In line over 50 mins. Whoever is in charge of logistics for the food staffing should go to Disney...see how they do things and do that here... I would definitely smooth things out. I applaud the Chick-fil-A station.... They had a better process.

The beverage stands had reasonable prices and I was pleasantly surprised.
We got to see quite a few animals.
I highly recommend the train ride...


The parking is out a ways...but I saw tram lines and teams taking people to outskirts parking lots. We had a handicap parking placard thanks to the in-laws... I am in firm belief that the overall planning and logistics of this park are professionally done and handled fairly well.

This venue is easy to get to if in the area and can go to a Busch gardens
The family had fun and that is what matters most!!
7/10 times I would return...
The sesame street experience and all the toddler rides didn't disappoint!

All in all if they smooth out a few things... I'm quite sure we would return. (Questions reach out)

Respectfully,

Bryan
Oklahoma City

original_text:
I've been an annual pass holder for the past 10 years. Keep in mind that this includes the BG in Virginia (which is sooo much better than this one!) I finally bit the bullet and canceled my pass to move on to Universal parks. The benefits of this pass have declined and it's no longer an attractive offer. First of all, you get preferred parking with your annual pass. However, 95 percent of the time I visit, they tell me the lot is full. Full? Really? Again? I started ignoring this and have ALWAYS been able to find a space in the preferred lot. Secondly, the pass no longer offers 50 percent discount on tickets. And lastly, you still get free guest tickets, but can only use one at a time per quarter.

The park itself is great, beautifully decorated during the holidays. The variety of shows is somewhat lacking, but the ones they do offer are excellent. The best one is the ice skating show. Love that they do pictures with Rudolph, the reindeer crew, at Christmas. The rides are thrilling and worth the wait in the lines. My favorite is iron gwazi.

Now, the onto what needs improving.... the food! The food is mediocre. Compared to the food at the BG Virginia where they offer cuisine appropriate to the country you're visiting (the theme there is the European countries), the food here is not theme park worthy. They do have some diverse offerings during food festivals, but they really should offer that all the time.

Bring the better benefits back to the passes, improve the food (maybe put in a couple nice full service restaurants) and stop lying to me about the preferred parking lot being full and maybe I'll consider coming back. Until then, I'm a universal pass holder

original_text:
My husband and I chose Busch gardens of all the Disney options in Florida because we wanted some fun rides and we got them :)

We arrived right at opening (10am) and left right at closing (5pm) in January. We definitely needed the whole time to see and do everything. We successfully did all the rides minus the Sesame Street kid land, the water ride that wasn't open, and one coaster that wasn't operating. We were running around the park to try and do everything and we barely made it to Zagora cafe to close out our all day dining pass with one final meal at 4:30 before the park closed. Note: Zagora was the only place open at this time, so if you are looking for food close to park closing don't do what we did and run in a circle around the park, go straight here.

The rides were a blast: I loved the iron gwazi the best, and the drop that tilts you to oook straight down before releasing into free fall. So we did those ones twice.

The longest line on a Tuesday at the end of January was about 20 minutes. Even then, we barely got all the rides in during the parks hours so there is plenty to do even though it didn't seem like it at first glance.

I also thoroughly enjoyed the animal exhibits. Normally I'm not into zoos but I felt good about the care of the animals here. The safari train ride was cool as were the bird aviaries. I really enjoyed the orangutans who were right up next to the view point saying hello to everyone.

It's a fun park because it mixed the animals encounters with the thrills for a diverse day.

They have a great team system from the parking lot. I would not park off the lot because the surrounding area is pretty sketchy feeling.

original_text:
I will say that Busch Gardens was definitely hard to do in one day. The placement of coasters/attractions are very far apart, with not a lot of food options in between, but plenty of strange carnival like games. Not too mention, it is safari hot here in Southwest Florida. Thank goodness for the train, if it's in service while you are there. About third of the rides were out of service.

I am grateful I won tickets with a parking pass so I only had to pay $45 for two orders of Chik-Fil-A nuggets, waffle fries, and drinks. Food definitely overpriced but it is a theme park. However, the food at the buffet was much more reasonably priced and more for your money than at Chik.

The employees were not too welcoming, and the train employees didn't seem to want to do their job keeping people safely away from entrances/exits. The employees on the coasters didn't seem to really care if you were locked in on the rides, which is quite concerning, especially when it is the Iron Gwazi.

Definitely overrated, overpriced, and disgusting. And why does everyone drink at theme parks down here? I get that it's five o' clock somewhere but you are seriously at a theme park, with your kids. Why do they make drinking and driving so easily obtainable here? Its a theme park, not a bar.

Would I go again? I do have two more passes but I think I'll just let them expire and save up for a trip to Universal or Disney.

Busch Gardens is a fun, educational, thrill seekers playground. From things like the Ice Show, to the endless tours and animal experiences (feed a Giraffe or a Kangaroo- you can here!), to some to thrill rides like Iron Gwazi, this park has something for everyone.

The Ambassadors were all friendly and smiling (maybe it's the magic of Christmas Town), and work so hard to keep areas clean and the ride's running smoothly. They have lots of locations to get drinks, which is helpful on a hot day. We really enjoy the down season during the weekday when you can go at your own pace, see what you want, ride what you want, and leave feeling happy and exhausted from a long day. If you have the opportunity to go during the weekday, you would be surprised by the difference it makes in the park.

original_text:
so this is a theme park mixed with a zoo. it's not cheap. it's big and not shaded. you pay for parking. food is like any other theme park, mediocre, bring your own food and water.
given that- if you carpool, come early and not in peak season you'll likely have a better time. LOTS of fun rides, lots of gift shops and lots of animals (cheetah, penguins, a safari train ride with zebra/rhino etc).
it's a unique place. parking and getting there is a pain. you park in the lot and wait in a long line for a big outdoor tram to take you to the gate. it's hot, and it's sunny. lots of walking.
it's a good place for teenager to go. small kids will like some of the rides and the elmo area and the animals.
if you plan on going for the day, review a map so you have an idea of how you want to navigate the park.
if you want to do all the rides etc and be relaxed- it's much better splitting up in 2 days.
i think it's a fun place if you know what you are getting into. it's definitely worth going for a fun time- but know it's not cheap and lots of walking in the sun.

original_text: I got tickets for myself and my children through the "Waves of Honor" program (I am a combat veteran). I was really looking forward to going there and it was my first trip to Tampa. Before I left I booked a preferred parking spot so I would be closer to the entrance; I have knee and back issues. We left Melbourne five hours before they opened (It takes three hours to get there) so we would be there well before they opened. When we got closer the traffic was horrendous. Really only one to get into the park. We waited for over an hour to get into the park. Once we arrived at the first booth, I explained that we had already paid for preferred parkin. The young lady looked surprised and said I dont't think you will get to park there; she recommended that I go to guest services to get my money back. As we proceeded to the parking lot we were directed to a parking lot that was not preferred parking. The girl was EXTREMELY rude and yelled at us to keep moving. In the meantime I watched two other cars get directed to the preferred parking. We were not allowed to park there even though I had paid for it and it was not cheap. As we were walking toward the shuttle/bus area I saw even more people parking there. The prices are outrageous and the lines move VERY slowly. Most of the employees were very rude. The sky ride and the safari are extra $$. Most of the rides were shut down. It is not like me to complain when something was free, but it was not enjoyable at all. I stood in line for an hour and 55 minutes to get my credit; that was time I could not spend with my family. It was suppose to be a relaxing day with my kids and it turned into the WORST experience I have ever had at an amusement park and I have been to quite a few. Guest services did refund my money for the parking and offered me free food and drink vouchers for my next visit. I declined the vouchers and advised them I will never return. The young lady could have cared less about my experience. This was the first time I have ever used a veteran benefit and it was a horrible experience. Next year I am going to Aquatica instead. Hopefully that will be a better experience.

original_text:
Rides are really fun and they have a great selection of coasters.

The giraffe experience was also a highlight, along with some of the bigger coasters. Bernie is awesome! Kind, gentle, curious, and very interactive. (I'm sure the other giraffes are also wonderful, but we spent our time with Bernie.) I do wish they didn't hit you with another $45/head, though. $15-25 per person would have been much more reasonable.

As for the food, don't expect much. It might have been better if the Oasis Pizza place hadn't been shut or if we hadn't missed the closing time for the BBQ place - two of the better reviewed restaurants at the park. So, maybe there is better food available than what we had.

The all-day food pass is worth while (especially if you can get it on sale for $30/head), but only because the prices are otherwise insane (i.e., pretty much what you'd expect at an amusement park). I'm serious though -- without the pass, a family of four could easily spend upwards of $160+ for a lunch of burgers and fries.

However, as a comparison, I would say that their food was infinitely better than the swill we paid dearly for in DisneyLand many years ago. Ugh. I'd have to make a concerted effort to make food that bad (Disney).

It's great to have a mix of animals and rides. Watching the cheetah training was also really neat (and no extra cost).

If you go during the off season, you'll likely have the run of the place. That's awesome. However, certain things will be closed or close early - not so good. You probably don't need the quick-pass during off season. We certainly did not. It's definitely worth going during off season, just know what to expect.

So much better than Disney's parks in so many ways and a much better value. I'm never going back to Disney.

This in no way to run a park. They still got our excessive $40 for parking and $180 admissions.
